mappings.rationaleAI: Imaginary numbers from 16th-century Italian algebraists through Euler's formalization, covering algebra history (A.2.e) and 18th-19th century developments (A.1.b.ii, A.1.b.iii).
Pure mathematics yielding applications: non-Euclidean geometry for relativity (A.2.d.iv), probability from games becoming statistics (A.3.a).
Probability theory from gambling through formalization: calculus of probability (A.3.a.i), alternative views (A.3.a.ii), asymptotic probabilities (A.3.a.iii), statistics (A.3.b).
European mathematics from Greek geometry through Arabic algebra to Newton/Leibniz calculus (A.1.b.i), covering geometry (A.2.d) and algebra (A.2.e).
Indian mathematicians: zero, decimal place-value (A.2.a.ii), Pythagorean theorem in Sulbasutras (A.2.d.i), westward transmission (A.2.e.ii).
Prime numbers from Euclid (A.1.a.ii) through Euler (A.1.b.ii) to Riemann's zeta function (A.1.b.iii).
Zeno's paradoxes: problems in Greek mathematics concerning infinity (A.1.a.ii, A.2.d.i), philosophical geometry (A.2.d.v), genetic method (B.2).
Euclid's Elements: Greek geometry and number theory (A.1.a.ii, A.2.d.i), philosophical aspects (A.2.d.v), Greek algebra (A.2.e.i), axiomatic method paradigm (B.1.a).
Infinity from Zeno through Cantor's transfinite cardinals: 19th-20th century developments (A.1.b.iii), philosophical geometry (A.2.d.v), topology/set theory (A.2.d.vi), cardinal numbers (B.2.b).

mappings.episodeTitle: Maths in the Early Islamic World
mappings.rationaleAI: Islamic mathematics: al-Khwarizmi's algebra, Omar Khayyam's cubics (A.2.e.iv), decimal notation (A.2.a.ii), transmission to Europe (A.1.a.iii).
Pythagoras: number as fundamental principle, right-angled triangle theorem (A.2.d.i), Greek number theory (A.2.e.i), philosophical foundations (B).
Zero from Indian/Arabic traditions through European adoption: positional numeral systems (A.2.a.ii), Islamic transmission (A.1.a.iii).
Logic history from Frege through Russell's Paradox to Gödel: axiomatic method (B.1.c), crisis in foundations (B.3.a), Logicism vs Formalism (B.3.c).
Negative numbers across civilizations: Chinese, Greek, Indian, Arabic, European — covering numeral concepts (A.2.a) and algebra traditions (A.2.e).
Pi from Archimedes to supercomputers: Greek geometry (A.2.d.i), Hellenistic mathematics (A.1.a.ii), computational methods (A.2.c.iii).
Symmetry through group theory by Galois: algebraic structures (A.2.e.iv), modern geometry (A.2.d.vi), 19th-20th century contributions (A.1.b.iii).
Russell's attempt to reduce mathematics to logic: Logicism (B.3.c), Russell's Paradox precipitating crisis in foundations (B.3.a).
Laplace advanced probability into systematic framework (A.3.a), relevant to 18th-century mathematics (A.1.b.ii).
Pythagorean connections between number and sound: Greek mathematical contributions (A.1.a.ii) and number theory (A.2.e.i).
Antikythera Mechanism: Hellenistic analogue computing device (A.1.a.ii) relevant to early computational instruments (A.2.c.ii).
Turing's On Computable Numbers is a foundational work in the history and foundations of mathematics, addressing computability and the limits of formal systems (A History, B Foundations).
Du Chatelet worked in 18th-century mathematics, contributing to advances in geometry, algebra, and analysis alongside contemporaries (A.1.b.ii).

Emmy Noether's founding role in modern algebra and Noether's Theorem belong to the development of mathematics in the 19th and 20th centuries, including contributions to the theories of groups and algebraic geometry (A.1.b.iii).

The episode addresses mathematics' foundational role and history from Galileo onward (A.1 on the development of mathematics through ancient, medieval, and modern times) and questions about the nature of mathematics as formalism vs. discovery connect to foundations of mathematics (B).
The episode traces mathematics' prehistoric origins in counting and measuring, connecting to the development of mathematics through ancient times (A.1).
